rationale: Frames your point as a bigger truth, so it lands as insight rather than criticism — and stays with them afterward.
core_sequence: Pattern or truth → elevated meaning → earned recommendation
primary_effect: Interpretive, wise, memorable — gives the audience a new frame for something familiar
failure_mode: Can over-sermonise or feel detached from practical reality if the truth is too abstract

rationale: Names their likely worry first, so they feel understood before the reframe — which lowers defensiveness and keeps them open.
core_sequence: Likely concern → humane validation → reframe → ask
primary_effect: Disarming, accurate, non-reactive — reduces defensiveness before making the point
failure_mode: Can become agreement theatre if the validation is too thick and the reframe too soft

rationale: Anchors your point to one concrete thing, so it's vivid and easy to grasp — and harder to brush off than an abstract claim.
core_sequence: Object or image → interpretation → practical consequence
primary_effect: Concrete, imagistic, sticky — makes the point land through something physical or visual
failure_mode: Can feel precious or theatrical if the image is forced or too ornate

core_sequence: Future residue → present tradeoff → action
primary_effect: Aspirational, motivating, time-aware — makes the present choice feel consequential
failure_mode: Can drift into hype or generic inspiration if the future scene is vague

rationale: Draws them into the thinking with a question, so the conclusion feels shared rather than imposed on them.
core_sequence: Reflective question → what matters most → recommendation
primary_effect: Participatory, clarifying, humane — draws the audience into the thinking before landing a point
failure_mode: Can become evasive if the recommendation never clearly arrives

rationale: Names the hard situation without blaming anyone, so the pressure drops and you can move forward together instead of fighting.
core_sequence: Acknowledge pressure → release accusation → systems view → path forward
primary_effect: Firm but non-punitive — names a hard situation without blame, then moves forward
failure_mode: Can become vague if the path forward is too soft or the acknowledgement too long

core_sequence: Compressed memorable line → short expansion → implication
primary_effect: Memorable, repeatable, scalable — creates a phrase people carry out of the room
failure_mode: Can become a slogan without substance if the expansion doesn't earn the line

core_sequence: Image or quote fragment → widened frame → grounded ask
primary_effect: Elevated but usable — lifts the thinking then returns to practical ground
failure_mode: Can become empty rhetoric if the grounded ask doesn't follow through

rationale: Says the hard thing clearly but opens softly, so the point fully lands without putting them on the defensive.
core_sequence: Gentle startup → owned feeling → clear direct point → next step
primary_effect: Honest without triggering defensiveness — the point lands fully but softly
failure_mode: Can feel cold if the gentleness is stripped, or weak if the directness is

rationale: Turns a vague complaint into an owned feeling, a clear example and a doable ask — so they can actually hear it and act.
core_sequence: Owned feeling → concrete example → request
primary_effect: Turns vague frustration or accusation into something hearable and actionable
failure_mode: Can become a case-file evidence dump if the example section is too long

rationale: Leads with ownership instead of excuses, so the apology feels real and the repair can actually begin.
core_sequence: Ownership → impact named → apology → repair step
primary_effect: Apology feels real and non-self-protective — leads with the effect not the excuse
failure_mode: Can become self-exonerating if explanation creeps before ownership

rationale: Builds feeling with vivid images, then lands on one plain phrase — so something tender hits with real emotional weight.
core_sequence: Three related sensory images → bridge sentence → plain key phrase
primary_effect: Sensory, vivid, emotionally alive — for love, longing, proposal, or devotion
failure_mode: Mixed emotional universes or an over-complicated landing destroy the effect

core_sequence: Name feeling → state rule clearly → safe alternative or next move
primary_effect: Softens the entry without softening the limit — regulation first, correction second
failure_mode: Boundary becomes unclear if feeling-mirror section is too long

core_sequence: State fact clearly → offer comfort → invite questions
primary_effect: Gives children accurate information with emotional safety — no misunderstanding risk
failure_mode: Comfort before fact on high-stakes truths creates confusion

core_sequence: Answer or recommendation first → brief narrative support → next step
primary_effect: Executive-ready clarity with enough context to make the recommendation credible
failure_mode: Narrative support becomes throat-clearing if it precedes the answer

core_sequence: Shared continuity → challenge or change → why-now → next step
primary_effect: Creates meaning and coherence under change — acknowledges what was before asking for what's next
failure_mode: Generic inspiration detached from the specific reality of the change

core_sequence: Mini-case or example → extracted principle → application
primary_effect: Makes the principle transferable and credible — grounded before it rises to abstraction
failure_mode: Case too long, principle too vague, or application too prescriptive

core_sequence: Recognize resistance → child-sized question or choice → guidance → next step
primary_effect: Uses participation without negotiating the non-negotiable — child feels heard and directed
failure_mode: Question becomes negotiation if the point is not clearly reached

stack_name: Perspective → Collaborative Frame → Consequence → Ownership Question
core_sequence: Perspective-first → collaborative frame → practical consequence → ownership question
primary_effect: Teen-safe: autonomous, non-babying, consequence-aware, participation-inviting
failure_mode: Becomes a lecture if perspective-first collapses into moralising

rationale: Builds the emotional ground first, so the key line arrives feeling earned and moving rather than dropped cold.
core_sequence: Build emotional truth → land phrase → optional echo
primary_effect: Deep, moving, reflective — phrase arrives after the audience has been prepared
failure_mode: Runway becomes too long and the landing loses impact

rationale: Eases in with a little context before the hard truth, so it's received instead of triggering a shutdown.
core_sequence: Soft context → emotional truth → clear point
primary_effect: Prepares a hard truth so it can be heard — runway lowers abruptness before the point lands
failure_mode: Padding buries the point; the runway becomes avoidance
source_doc: Relationship Communication Master Reference v2.0 (App.B: abrupt truth → runway + truth)

rationale: Opens calmly to set the tone, because the first sentence largely decides whether the talk goes well or turns defensive.
core_sequence: Soft opening (importance + calm intent) → the point
primary_effect: Lowers the defensive trajectory of a hard conversation — the opening sets the tone
failure_mode: Over-padded opening that buries or delays the actual point
source_doc: Relationship Communication Master Reference v2.0 (App.B: harsh → gentle startup, §8)

rationale: Trades attack-language for the fear underneath, so they meet your need instead of defending against a criticism.
core_sequence: Name the reaction → underlying fear → what you are needing
primary_effect: Converts attack-shaped language into owned vulnerability the other person can meet
failure_mode: Tips into needy pressure if the need becomes a demand for reassurance
source_doc: Relationship Communication Master Reference v2.0 (§4, App.B: protest → vulnerable need)

rationale: Asks instead of demands, so they can say yes freely — which makes real cooperation far more likely.
core_sequence: Observation → specific doable ask → “would you be willing…”
primary_effect: Cooperative and non-coercive — a request that leaves the other person free to say yes
failure_mode: Too soft when a firm non-negotiable limit is actually required
source_doc: Relationship Communication Master Reference v2.0 (App.B: demand → request)

rationale: Centres the apology on how it affected them, not on your guilt — so it reads as real remorse and they feel understood.
core_sequence: “I’m sorry” → specific impact named → understanding of why it hurt
primary_effect: Remorse centred on the other person, not on the speaker's guilt
failure_mode: Self-focused apology drift (“I feel awful, I hate myself”) that makes them comfort you
source_doc: Relationship Communication Master Reference v2.0 (App.B: self-focused → impact apology)

citation: Laurenceau et al. (1998); Arican-Dinc et al. (2023)
rationale: Owns the mistake and steadies the bond at once, so they hear the repair without fearing the relationship is in question.
core_sequence: Own it → impact → apology → reassurance of care and continuity
primary_effect: Repairs the rupture and steadies the bond at the same time
failure_mode: Reassurance softens or dilutes the accountability it is paired with
source_doc: Relationship Communication Master Reference v2.0 (output-pair logic)

rationale: States the limit without guilt or over-explaining, so it stays firm and clear instead of getting talked out of.
core_sequence: Guilt-stripped limit → what you will/won’t do → next step
primary_effect: Firm and dignified — the limit lands without self-blame or apology weakening it
failure_mode: Can read cold if no warmth is present at all
source_doc: Relationship Communication Master Reference v2.0 (App.B: boundary-with-guilt → clean boundary)

rationale: Holds the limit while signalling care, so a 'no' doesn't feel like rejection or abandonment.
core_sequence: Boundary → care and continuity → next move
primary_effect: Sets a limit without signalling abandonment — the relationship survives the no
failure_mode: Over-explaining the care weakens the limit
source_doc: Relationship Communication Master Reference v2.0 (output-pair logic)

rationale: Swaps borrowed therapy-speak for plain, honest words, so it sounds like you — and lands as sincere, not performed.
core_sequence: Strip therapy-speak → plain, owned statement
primary_effect: Authentic and un-performative — replaces borrowed clinical diction with real words
failure_mode: Can feel blunt if stripped so far that warmth is lost
source_doc: Relationship Communication Master Reference v2.0 (App.B: over-therapized → plain)

rationale: Ties thanks to a specific moment, so gratitude feels real and memorable instead of generic and forgettable.
core_sequence: Specific moment → what it meant → why it mattered
primary_effect: Concrete, memorable gratitude — specificity makes thanks land instead of sounding generic
failure_mode: Inflates a small thing into something it wasn't
source_doc: Relationship Communication Master Reference v2.0 (App.B: generic → appreciation story)

citation: Patterson et al., Crucial Conversations (Start with Heart)
rationale: Shows you get why it's hard and makes your good intent visible, so a braced listener relaxes enough to hear the ask.
core_sequence: “I can see why this is hard” → “what I want here is…” → “could we…”
primary_effect: Disarms a braced listener — validation and visible motive restore safety before the ask
failure_mode: The motive line becomes a cover for control if it doesn't match behaviour
source_doc: Inspiration Corpus Structured Analysis (recipe 1) + Source Pack (Start with Heart)

rationale: Frames it as the two of you versus the problem, so it becomes joint problem-solving instead of an accusation.
core_sequence: Name the shared issue → separate identity from the issue → “how do we solve this?”
primary_effect: Turns conflict into joint problem-solving — it's us vs the problem, not us vs each other
failure_mode: Can feel cold or over-rational if the emotional reality is skipped

rationale: Paints a hopeful shared future and invites them toward it, so a hard conversation points somewhere good.
core_sequence: Vivid shared future → gap with the present → “let’s…”
primary_effect: Mobilising and hopeful — makes a desired direction feel real and reachable together
failure_mode: Drifts into pressure or hype if the future scene is idealised

rationale: Admits there's no easy way to say it, then offers a dignified next step — so hard news lands with care, not coldness.
core_sequence: “There’s no easy way to say this” → clarify the reality → dignified next step
primary_effect: Delivers hard news with dignity — names the difficulty honestly, then offers a path
failure_mode: Reaches for meaning or consolation too soon and instrumentalises the pain
source_doc: Inspiration Corpus Structured Analysis (recipe 7) + Source Pack (Bixby/Stripe/Airbnb)

rationale: Leads with an observation, not a verdict, so feedback about a habit lands as help rather than an attack.
core_sequence: Observation (not a verdict) → reflective bridge → name the behaviour → its consequence → requested change
primary_effect: Feedback on a recurring behaviour without it landing as an attack
failure_mode: Too many beats; can feel managed or clinical
source_doc: Leadership / Coaching / Teaching / Speaking Master Reference v2.0 (feedback best-mode)

rationale: Asks rather than tells, so they arrive at the insight themselves — which sticks far better than being lectured.
core_sequence: Open question → reflection → ownership question
primary_effect: Lets the other person self-generate the insight rather than being told
failure_mode: Becomes evasive if the speaker withholds their own position entirely
source_doc: Leadership / Coaching / Teaching / Speaking Master Reference v2.0 (coaching mode)

rationale: Anchors what you've built before naming the hard change, so it steadies you both through a rough patch.
core_sequence: Anchor what you’ve built / are → name the hard change → next move together
primary_effect: Steadies the relationship during change or conflict by leading with continuity
failure_mode: Continuity is used to dodge or soften the hard point
source_doc: Leadership / Coaching / Teaching / Speaking Master Reference v2.0 (context → continuity → challenge), adapted

rationale: Asks before sharing something tender, so they're ready to receive it — which makes a sensitive reveal feel safe.
core_sequence: Ask to say something → brief emotional runway → land the key phrase
primary_effect: Stages a tender or sensitive reveal so the other person is ready to receive it
failure_mode: Ceremony weakens impact; overuse makes ordinary messages feel theatrical
source_doc: Storytelling Master Reference v2.0 (staging operators) + Elevated Inspiration Playbook

rationale: Names their likely reaction before they feel it, which disarms defensiveness and opens a real conversation.
core_sequence: Pre-name their likely reaction → label the emotion → no-pressure “how do we…” question
primary_effect: Defuses defensiveness by naming the negative first and inviting collaboration
failure_mode: Reads as sarcasm or manipulation if mistimed or under-set-up
source_doc: Inspiration Corpus Source Pack (D4: Voss calibrated question / labeling)

rationale: Says the actual point first, then a line of why — so an anxious over-explainer gets heard instead of lost in preamble.
core_sequence: State the point or ask first → one line of context
primary_effect: Anti-throat-clearing clarity — says the thing plainly for an anxious over-explainer
failure_mode: Can feel abrupt for tender or high-emotion topics
source_doc: Business Communication Master Reference v2.0 (answer-first / BLUF), adapted
